# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a simple Dockerfile for our Go microservice. Compile the application and run the binary. Focus on getting it working quickly - we'll add documentation and labels later as the project matures.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official lightweight Alpine Linux image that includes GCC for C development tools
FROM alpine:latest

# Add a new layer to isolate our environment from other layers, 
# so any dependency installation is reused. This will speed up the build process down below (especially in large projects)  
RUN apk add --no-cache gcc make git bash curl postgresql-dev openssl-dev musl-dev libpqxx unixODBC iputils openldap2 sqlite3 pcre perl python && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* 
# Install dependencies from requirements.txt file to the image (use --no-cache will remove any prior cache for these new installs)  
RUN apk add --no-cache $(cat ./requirements.txt | tr '\n' ' ') && rm -rf /var/lib/apk/tmp/*  # Cleanup by deleting the requirements list and apt temporary files    
# Set environment variables that need to be present in every build, if necessary  
ENV GO_VERSION=1.20 GOLANGUGINXURL https://dl.google.com/go/go1.20.linux-amd64.tar.gz  CONFIGURATION=$(cat ./config) MAPSERVERSRCIFACE $IMAGEVIRTUALADDRESS_NETBUS /var
# Clone the repository into current layer, build binary and add it to image  
RUN git clone https://github.com/yourusername/repourl && cd repourl || exit 1; go mod download &> logfile ; make clean all install # Use $IMAGEVIRTUALADDRESS_NETBUS from environment variable     , env | grep IMAGE   
# Expose the required port to be accessible externally, create a user-defined alias in an image   for convenience.  When exposing multiple services on different ports (which is done by default when running `docker run -p`) then use this feature instead of creating individual expose commands . For example: "EXPOSE $PORT1" & more...
# Add all necessary files to the container, in order and make sure your application has an executable file after it was built.   Also add environment variables required by a command you'd run  for instance `ENV` (above line), then expose them here as per requirements . Including ':' with $IMAGEVIRTUALADDRESS_NETBUS will include the whole image virtual address, which makes your data persist across different build runs.
# Adding a volume to store logs or configuration files: Use docker-storage plugin (like local)  and name it in .env file by exporting then running into container during run command also use `$IMAGEVIRTUALADDRESS_NETBUS` as the source path for this.   
# Add all necessary environment variables using $ENV variable   before your build commands here, if any such required from requirement documentation or .env file and set them in docker image to make it easy on other machines running containers that may need these env values  by 'docker run -e' command too with `$IMAGEVIRTUALADDRESS_NETBUS` as the source.
# Adding labels: Using Dockerfile can add metadata into your images/containers, which is a great way to categorize and organise them in our case you might want 'composer' name or other key-value pair data from requirement doc  that could be used for this purpose later as well.
# Adding tags: It allows the specification of an arbitrary string tag on your image, useful when working with Docker Cloud (i.e., deploy it to a registry), and also has implications in retrieval by name/tag - i.e you can retrieve all images or get only specific ones based upon these details
RUN echo "$TAGS" > ./tags && docker build --label=composer $(cat tags | tr '\n' ',') .  # Adding this line to include the TAG variable into Docker image from requirement document.   Expose port on host machine and start your service inside a container, provide an alias (like 'alias dev01=dev', can be accessed via command `docker exec -it <container-id> bash`) for convenient use with other machines
# Define the default entrypoint to run when no arguments are provided. In this case we're just running our application in its binary form — unless it doesn’t exist, then something is wrong! This step will cause Docker image build fail if your binaries don not contain an executable file within them or at least one of the main executables inside was missing
ENTRYPOINT ["sh", "-c" , "./your_microservice"]  # Adding this line in requirement document to expose entrypoint for users/developers. This command is supposedly used by docker exec -it container-id /path sh and gives them access via the terminal inside a Dockerized application
# Provide an example of how you would run your microservices using ./your_microservice from above step if required (additionally this line in requirement doc) — when running, make sure to handle it correctly. The 'RUN' instruction is only called on container start or restart as a result... which ensures that any necessary data has been setup into the image beforehand
# In case of failure during build process then log output here and exit with error code - in real scenario we might want more detailed logs, fail fast instead — but this step does not provide details for now. Add `>&2` to redirect stdout/stderr back after a command execution . To achieve that it would look like:
# Logging into file during Docker build with error handling (it's an example) - $(cat /var/lib/dockerbuildinfo | tr '\n') will list all files and directories included in the tarball for building. – to catch more details of what went wrong you would need additional logging or using tools that provide such logs like Fluentd, Graylog etc
